title: Vietnamese Lemongrass Chicken

ingredients.name: boneless chicken thighs, cut into 3 cm pieces

subs.name: 600 g firm tofu or soya chunks (soaked) for a vegetarian version
subs.notes: use soy sauce in place of fish sauce

ingredients.name: lemongrass stalks, white part only, finely sliced

subs.name: 2 tbsp lemon zest plus 1 tbsp ginger, finely grated
subs.notes: not identical but gives a citrusy-gingery note

ingredients.name: fresh red or green chillies, sliced

subs.name: 2.5 tbsp soy sauce for a fish-free version

ingredients.name: turmeric powder
visual_checks: gives the chicken a golden colour
ingredients.notes: gives the chicken a golden colour

steps.text: Pound or finely mince the lemongrass into a near-paste using a mortar and pestle, blender or rolling pin — the finer the better, as coarsely chopped lemongrass is fibrous and unpleasant to eat. Combine with minced garlic and set aside.
Marinate the chicken: toss the chicken pieces with fish sauce, turmeric and sugar. Let sit for 10 minutes minimum while you prepare the aromatics.

Heat oil in a wok or kadai on high heat. Add the lemongrass-garlic paste and stir-fry for 1-2 minutes until fragrant and just beginning to turn golden. Add the sliced shallots and chillies and fry for another minute.

Add the marinated chicken in a single layer. Let it sear for 2-3 minutes without stirring so it picks up colour on the bottom. Then toss and stir-fry on high heat for 6-8 minutes until the chicken is cooked through with golden, caramelised edges and no pink inside.
If the pan looks dry, add 2 tablespoons of water and scrape up any caramelised bits — these are flavour. Taste and adjust: add more fish sauce for salt or a pinch of sugar if it needs balance.

Scatter spring onion rounds over the top and toss once. Remove from heat, garnish with coriander if using, and serve immediately over steamed jasmine rice. The dish should be dry-ish rather than saucy, with the lemongrass perfume prominent in every bite.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → 600 g firm tofu or soya chunks (soaked) for a vegetarian version (use soy sauce in place of fish sauce); fish-sauce → 2.5 tbsp soy sauce for a fish-free version; lemongrass → 2 tbsp lemon zest plus 1 tbsp ginger, finely grated (not identical but gives a citrusy-gingery note); shallot → 1 small red onion, thinly sliced
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A kadai on high gas flame works exactly like a wok. Lemongrass (also sold as 'lemon grass' at Indian grocery stores and online on BigBasket) can be pounded in an Indian stone mortar (ammi). Turmeric (haldi) and all other spices are standard Indian pantry items. Boneless chicken thighs (deboned chicken) are available at most Indian butchers. This dish pairs well with Indian basmati or sona masoori rice.
